title: EvidenceSpectral: Random Matrix Theory Reveals Two Signal Dimensions in Cochrane Trust Component Correlations
author: Mahmood Ahmad
primary_estimand: Number of signal eigenvalues above Marchenko-Pastur upper bound
summary: Random matrix theory decomposes trust component correlations across 6,229 Cochrane meta-analyses into two signal dimensions, rejecting the largest eigenvalue as random noise with a Tracy-Widom statistic of 77.9.
body: Do five trust components of Cochrane meta-analyses share meaningful latent structure, or are their correlations indistinguishable from random noise? We applied random matrix theory to the 5x5 Pearson correlation matrix of audit, consistency, robustness, stability, and power scores across 6,229 Cochrane systematic reviews. Marchenko-Pastur theory predicts that for p=5 variables and n=6,229 observations, noise eigenvalues fall within [0.944, 1.057]; any eigenvalue above 1.057 constitutes a signal dimension. We identified two signal eigenvalues (1.841 and 1.137), a global quality axis loading on robustness, audit, and power, and a precision-versus-consistency axis, together explaining 59.6% of total variance. The largest eigenvalue was rejected as noise by a Tracy-Widom statistic of s=77.9 (p<10^-100), with participation ratio 3.4 confirming broad loading across three components rather than a single-component artefact. Trust structure in Cochrane evidence is genuinely two-dimensional, not a single latent quality continuum. Applications to dimensionality-reduced scoring and domain-stratified weighting are limited by the cross-sectional design and absence of outcome validation.

sentences.text: Do five trust components of Cochrane meta-analyses share meaningful latent structure, or are their correlations indistinguishable from random noise?
We applied random matrix theory to the 5x5 Pearson correlation matrix of audit, consistency, robustness, stability, and power scores across 6,229 Cochrane systematic reviews.
Marchenko-Pastur theory predicts that for p=5 variables and n=6,229 observations, noise eigenvalues fall within [0.944, 1.057]; any eigenvalue above 1.057 constitutes a signal dimension.

We identified two signal eigenvalues (1.841 and 1.137), a global quality axis loading on robustness, audit, and power, and a precision-versus-consistency axis, together explaining 59.6% of total variance.

The largest eigenvalue was rejected as noise by a Tracy-Widom statistic of s=77.9 (p<10^-100), with participation ratio 3.4 confirming broad loading across three components rather than a single-component artefact.
Trust structure in Cochrane evidence is genuinely two-dimensional, not a single latent quality continuum.
Applications to dimensionality-reduced scoring and domain-stratified weighting are limited by the cross-sectional design and absence of outcome validation.

notes.data: 6,229 Cochrane meta-analyses trust component scores (EvidenceScore v1.0)

checks.name: result sentence has estimand
checks.detail: Sentence 4 names signal eigenvalues as primary estimand.

search_strategy.summary: All 6,229 Cochrane systematic reviews with complete EvidenceScore trust component profiles. No literature search required; analysis is performed on an existing scored dataset from the MetaAudit pipeline.
filters: complete trust component scores required (no missing values)
queries: scores.csv: all rows with non-null audit_score, consistency_score, robustness_score, stability_score, power_score

reasons_excluded.reason: Missing one or more component scores

analysis_modules.label: Marchenko-Pastur Bounds
analysis_modules.summary: For p=5 variables and n=6,229 observations, gamma=0.000803, lambda+=1.057, lambda-=0.944.

analysis_modules.label: Eigenvalue Spectrum
analysis_modules.summary: Five eigenvalues: 1.841 (signal), 1.137 (signal), 0.905, 0.676, 0.441. Two exceed the MP upper bound.

analysis_modules.label: Tracy-Widom Test
analysis_modules.summary: The largest eigenvalue (1.841) yields s=77.9, decisively rejecting the null hypothesis of random noise.

analysis_modules.label: First Eigenvector Loadings
analysis_modules.summary: EV1 loads strongly on robustness (+0.587), audit (+0.508), and stability (+0.216); consistency (+0.166) loads moderately.
